fragment:clb-support-kit:lesson-planning.normalizedText: effective planning is learner-centred, task-based, develops five components of language ability, and uses authentic texts
fragment:clb-support-kit:lesson-planning.summary: Reference fragment for task-authoring validation and template design.
fragment:clb-support-kit:lesson-planning.text: Effective planning is learner-centred, focuses on real-life communication tasks, develops grammatical, textual, sociolinguistic, functional, and strategic abilities, and uses authentic texts and resources learners may encounter outside the classroom.
fragment:clb-support-kit:lesson-planning.title: Support Kit lesson-planning characteristics
fragment:clb-support-kit:lesson-planning.validationNotes: Use as validation basis for learner-centred and authentic-task checks.

fragment:clb-support-kit:task-analysis:0.normalizedText: refer to features of communication; do task analysis to isolate discrete task components; components become skill-building activities; some skills become assessment criteria; indicators and profile of ability can be used as criteria
sectionPath: Using the CLB for Classroom Planning
fragment:clb-support-kit:task-analysis:0.summary: Key fragment for deriving wizard supports and rubric-generation logic.
fragment:clb-support-kit:task-analysis:0.text: When creating a classroom task, instructors should refer to features of communication and then do a task analysis to isolate discrete task components. These components become the basis for skill-building activities. Some of these skills may also serve as assessment criteria later. Indicators of ability and generic indicators in the Profile of Ability can also be used as criteria for assessing how well learners complete the task.
fragment:clb-support-kit:task-analysis:0.title: Support Kit task analysis and assessment criteria
fragment:clb-support-kit:task-analysis:0.validationNotes: Use to justify criteriaLibrary and task-analysis step.

fragment:clb-support-kit:task-planning-p.normalizedText: start with real-world task; relate task to clb competency; plan skill-building and skill-using activities; develop assessment task at end of lesson sequence
sectionPath: Using the CLB for Classroom Planning
fragment:clb-support-kit:task-planning-p.summary: Core planning sequence for the future task wizard.
fragment:clb-support-kit:task-planning-p.text: A planning process can begin with a real-world task, relate it to a CLB competency, plan skill-building and skill-using activities, and then develop an assessment task so learners can demonstrate what they can do at the end of the lesson sequence.
fragment:clb-support-kit:task-planning-p.title: Support Kit task-planning process
fragment:clb-support-kit:task-planning-p.validationNotes: Use as primary source for wizard step order.

fragment:clb:2012:competency-areas:001.normalizedText: competency areas include comprehending instructions giving instructions getting things done comprehending information sharing information and reproducing information; competency statements are general statements of communicative language ability
fragment:clb:2012:competency-areas:001.summary: Starter definition fragment for populating competency-area reference data.
fragment:clb:2012:competency-areas:001.text: Competency Areas include Comprehending Instructions, Giving Instructions, Getting Things Done, Comprehending Information, Sharing Information, and Reproducing Information. Competency Statements are general statements of communicative language ability associated with each competency area and benchmark.
fragment:clb:2012:competency-areas:001.title: CLB competency area definitions
fragment:clb:2012:competency-areas:001.validationNotes: Use to seed competency-area families and definitions.

fragment:clb:2012:framework-definition:0.normalizedText: each benchmark includes a profile of ability competency statements and sample indicators of ability; sample tasks illustrate competency statements in real-world contexts; competency statements are general statements of communicative language ability; indicators depend on purpose and context; sample tasks do not define a benchmark; features of communication help identify level-appropriate tasks
fragment:clb:2012:framework-definition:0.summary: Canonical CLB explanation of benchmark components and how sample tasks and features of communication should be used.
fragment:clb:2012:framework-definition:0.text: Each benchmark includes a Profile of Ability, Competency Statements and Sample Indicators of Ability. Sample Tasks illustrate the Competency Statements in real-world contexts. Competency Statements are general statements of communicative language ability. Sample Indicators provide a general indication of what a person might need to do for authentic tasks, but actual indicators depend on the purpose and context of communication. Sample Tasks do not define a benchmark. Some Features of Communication across a stage can help identify level-appropriate tasks for instructional or assessment purposes.
fragment:clb:2012:framework-definition:0.title: CLB framework structure definition
fragment:clb:2012:framework-definition:0.validationNotes: Use as root citation for benchmark/competency/indicator/sample-task relationships.

fragment:clb:2012:speaking:1:profile:001.normalizedText: the speaker can: communicate basic personal information using short phrases and some sentences, usually in response to questions about personal needs and experiences. when the communication is:  short and face-to-face  strongly supported by gestures and visual cues  informal  with one familiar person at a time  guided and encouraged by questions and prompts from a highly supportive listener  in non-demanding contexts demonstrating these strengths and limitations:  speaks in short phrases and some short sentences, with very little evidence of connected discourse  fluency is not adequate to sustain simple conversations; speech rate is slow with frequent pauses and hesitations  vocabulary is limited for basic everyday, routine communication  little control over basic grammar structures and tenses  grammar, vocabulary and pronunciation difficulties may significantly impede communication  relies on gestures

fragment:clb:2012:writing:4:profile:001.text: The writer can: Write short, simple to moderately complex descriptions, narrations and communications about familiar, concrete topics related to daily life and experience. When the communication is:  On a familiar and personally relevant topic  Intended for a familiar audience  Relatively short  In moderately demanding contexts Demonstrating these strengths and limitations:  Adequate paragraph structure with a main idea and some supporting details  Adequate use of connective words and phrases  Adequate range of vocabulary for most simple everyday texts  Good control of simple structures  Difficulty with complex structures  Adequate control of spelling, punctuation and format  Some awkward-sounding phrases and word combinations  Able to communicate some moderately complex messages
fragment:clb:2012:writing:4:profile:001.title: Writing Benchmark 4 Profile of Ability

fragment:elahe:productive-feedback:clb-s.normalizedText: criteria should meet but not exceed clb standard; words like correctly and competently may exceed adequate control; phrase criteria positively and in learner-friendly language
fragment:elahe:productive-feedback:clb-s.summary: Validation rule seed for wording checks in rubrics.
fragment:elahe:productive-feedback:clb-s.text: Criteria should meet but not exceed the CLB standard. Wording like correctly or competently can exceed the Profile of Ability wording such as adequate control. Criteria should be phrased positively and in learner-friendly language.
fragment:elahe:productive-feedback:clb-s.title: Productive feedback on wording that exceeds CLB standard
fragment:elahe:productive-feedback:clb-s.validationNotes: Turn into warning rules for forbidden wording and positive phrasing.

fragment:elahe:productive-feedback:holis.normalizedText: holistic criterion should be one statement focused on communicative success only; do not mix analytic criteria into holistic; include competency-area criteria and control-of-structures criterion in learner-friendly language
fragment:elahe:productive-feedback:holis.notes: Derived from facilitator feedback on PBLA productive task design.
fragment:elahe:productive-feedback:holis.summary: Validation rule seed for productive-task rubric structure.
fragment:elahe:productive-feedback:holis.text: A holistic criterion should be one statement focused on success in communication only. It should not summarize the task and should not contain analytic criteria. Criteria from the competency area should be included as part of the assessment in learner-friendly language, along with a criterion related to control over structures from the Profile of Ability.
fragment:elahe:productive-feedback:holis.title: Productive feedback on holistic vs analytic criteria
fragment:elahe:productive-feedback:holis.validationNotes: Turn into warning rule: holistic cannot contain analytic content.

fragment:elahe:productive-feedback:succe.normalizedText: success should reflect key clb criteria for real-world success, not only score; mark key criteria; include competency-area and control-of-structures criteria; numeric success threshold should be 70 percent or more
fragment:elahe:productive-feedback:succe.summary: Validation rule seed for success-policy logic.
fragment:elahe:productive-feedback:succe.text: A measure of success should reflect necessary CLB criteria for success in the real world and at the CLB level, not just a score. Key criteria should be starred or otherwise indicated. Critical items include criteria from the competency area and a criterion on control of structures. If using a numerical score, the minimum for success in PBLA is 70 percent or more.
fragment:elahe:productive-feedback:succe.title: Productive feedback on success measures
fragment:elahe:productive-feedback:succe.validationNotes: Use for successPolicies and wizard validation.

fragment:elahe:receptive-feedback:succes.normalizedText: receptive success should reflect necessary clb criteria for real-world success and level, not only question score; indicate key criteria
fragment:elahe:receptive-feedback:succes.summary: Validation rule seed for receptive success logic.
fragment:elahe:receptive-feedback:succes.text: For receptive tasks, successful completion should reflect achievement of the necessary CLB criteria for real-world success and the CLB level, not only whether specific questions are correct. The measure of success should indicate which criteria are key for success.
fragment:elahe:receptive-feedback:succes.title: Receptive feedback on success measures
fragment:elahe:receptive-feedback:succes.validationNotes: Use for successPolicies on receptive tasks.

fragment:elahe:receptive-feedback:task-s.normalizedText: receptive comprehension questions should align with identified clb criteria and level; task sheet should include clear instructions, correct competency area, planning-file criteria, and note that spelling grammar and punctuation are not scored
fragment:elahe:receptive-feedback:task-s.summary: Validation rule seed for receptive task handouts.
fragment:elahe:receptive-feedback:task-s.text: For receptive assessment tasks, comprehension questions should align with the identified CLB criteria and be suitable for the level. The learner task sheet should include clear instructions, the correct competency area, the assessment criteria from the planning file, and a note that spelling, grammar, and punctuation will not be scored.
fragment:elahe:receptive-feedback:task-s.title: Receptive feedback on task-sheet wording and learner instructions
fragment:elahe:receptive-feedback:task-s.validationNotes: Use to create receptive-task handout checks.

fragment:elahe:receptive-notes:landlord-.normalizedText: sample receptive task reading a message from a landlord at clb 4; competency area getting things done; criteria mapped to question numbers; include note that spelling grammar and punctuation are not scored
sectionPath: Reading a Message from a Landlord
fragment:elahe:receptive-notes:landlord-.summary: Seed example for receptive task-family and criterion mapping.
fragment:elahe:receptive-notes:landlord-.text: Sample receptive assessment task: Reading a Message from a Landlord, CLB 4, Competency Area Getting Things Done. Criteria are linked to question numbers: identify purpose, identify key information or details, make inferences, and understand the message to take appropriate action. The notes also indicate that the instructions must tell learners that spelling, grammar, and punctuation will not be scored.
fragment:elahe:receptive-notes:landlord-.title: Receptive landlord-message task example
fragment:elahe:receptive-notes:landlord-.validationNotes: Use later for receptive task-family example records.
